request.question: "Could I Leave You?" was a song in Follies, a musical concering what?

title: The Fabulous Palm Springs Follies
Follies
Ziegfeld Follies of 1936
Could I Leave You? (song)
Ziegfeld Follies (film)
Fox Movietone Follies of 1929
Rock Follies
Mark Rodenhauser
Glorifying the American Girl
Our Gang Follies of 1938
sentences: The Fabulous Palm Springs Follies was a Ziegfeld Follies style dance and musical review show that played at the historic Plaza Theatre in Palm Springs, California, United States, seasonally from November to mid-May.
The Follies was founded in 1990 by Riff Markowitz and Mary Jardin.
Impresario Markowitz also served as the show's managing director and emcee.
The show was often credited with helping to revitalize and maintain the downtown area by bringing in patrons from around the globe.
The Follies was unique in that it only featured performers 55 and older and holds Guinness World Records for this claim to fame.
It was the subject of a short documentary titled "" which was nominated at the 70th Academy Awards for Best Short Subject Documentary.
A segment that aired on Seattle television station KOMO-TV that featured the Follies received an Emmy in 1997.
The shows attracted approximately 170,000 attendees yearly.
On June 5, 2013, co-founders Markowitz and Jardin announced they would close the Follies on May 18, 2014.
Follies is a musical with music and lyrics by Stephen Sondheim and a book by James Goldman.

The story concerns a reunion in a crumbling Broadway theatre, scheduled for demolition, of the past performers of the "Weismann's Follies", a musical revue (based on the "Ziegfeld Follies"), that played in that theatre between the World Wars.
It focuses on two couples, Buddy and Sally Durant Plummer and Benjamin and Phyllis Rogers Stone, who are attending the reunion.
Sally and Phyllis were showgirls in the Follies.
Both couples are deeply unhappy with their marriages.

Buddy, a traveling salesman, is having an affair with a girl on the road; Sally is still as much in love with Ben as she was years ago; and Ben is so self-absorbed that Phyllis feels emotionally abandoned.
Several of the former showgirls perform their old numbers, sometimes accompanied by the ghosts of their former selves.
The musical numbers in the show have been interpreted as pastiches of the styles of the leading Broadway composers of the 1920s ands '30s, and sometimes as parodies of specific songs.
The Ziegfeld Follies of 1936 is a musical revue with lyrics by Ira Gershwin, music by Vernon Duke and sketches by Gershwin and David Freedman.
The Ziegfeld Follies were a series of revues presented from 1907 through 1931, 1934, 1936, 1943, and 1957.
"Could I Leave You?"
is a song written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical "Follies" for the character Phyllis.

Ziegfeld Follies is a 1946 American musical comedy film released by Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer and directed by Lemuel Ayers, Roy Del Ruth, Robert Lewis, Vincente Minnelli, Merrill Pye, George Sidney and Charles Walters.

It stars many of MGM leading talents, including Fred Astaire, Lucille Ball, Lucille Bremer, Fanny Brice (the only member of the ensemble who was a star of the original Follies), Judy Garland, Kathryn Grayson, Lena Horne, Gene Kelly, James Melton, Victor Moore, William Powell, Red Skelton, and Esther Williams.

Fox Movietone Follies of 1929, also known as Movietone Follies of 1929 and The William Fox Movietone Follies of 1929, was a black-and-white and color American musical film released by Fox Film Corporation.
Rock Follies, and its sequel, Rock Follies of '77, is a musical drama shown on British television in the 1970s.
The storyline followed the ups and downs of a fictional female rock band called the "Little Ladies" as they struggled for recognition and success.

The series starred Rula Lenska, Charlotte Cornwell and Julie Covington as the Little Ladies, with support from Emlyn Price, Beth Porter, Sue Jones-Davies, Bob Hoskins, Stephen Moore, Derek Thompson, Denis Lawson and Little Nell among others.
The series was made with a very low budget for Thames Television, with a style inspired by fringe theatre.
Mark Todd Rodenhauser (born June 1, 1961 in Elmhurst, Illinois) is a former American football center who played 13 seasons in the National Football League with seven different teams.
He played college football at Illinois State University.
He was selected by the Carolina Panthers in the 1995 NFL Expansion Draft.
Rodenhauser started his football career with the Chicago Bruisers of the Arena Football League.
He also made an appearance in the popular series Football Follies, in the 21st Century Follies DVDs, where he could snap a football a full length of a basketball court.
Glorifying the American Girl is a 1929 American Pre-Code, musical comedy film produced by Florenz Ziegfeld that highlights Ziegfeld Follies performers.
The last third of the film (which was filmed in early Technicolor) is basically a Follies production, with cameo appearances by Rudy Vallee, Helen Morgan, and Eddie Cantor.
Our Gang Follies of 1938 (later reissued as simply "Follies of 1938") is a 1937 American musical short subject, the 161st short subject entry in Hal Roach's "Our Gang" ("Little Rascals") series.
Directed by Gordon Douglas as a sequel to 1935's "Our Gang Follies of 1936", the two-reel short was released to theaters on December 18, 1937 by Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: "Could I Leave You?" was a song in Follies, a musical concering what?
hop1_docs: Could I Leave You? (song) | "Could I Leave You?"  is a song written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical "Follies" for the character Phyllis.

When You Leave That Way You Can Never Go Back | "When You Leave That Way You Can Never Go Back" is a song written by Steve Clark and Johnny MacRae.  The song -- a bittersweet reflection of a condemned inmate's life, looking back at all the bridges he burned and wished could be repaired -- was recorded by several country music artists, including Sam Neely, Bill Anderson and the band Confederate Railroad.
What You Leave Behind (album) | What You Leave Behind is the third studio album by the American country music band Ricochet.  It was released in 2000 on Columbia Records.

Give You What You Like | "Give You What You Like" is a song recorded by Canadian singer-songwriter Avril Lavigne from her self-titled fifth studio album released in 2013.  It was written by Lavigne with her husband Chad Kroeger and David Hodges.  The song has been praised by critics for expanding Lavigne's musical diversity and exposing her to new genres of music and is the sixth track on the album.  Musically, "Give You What You Like" is a folk-pop song with lyrics that describe combating loneliness through swapping sex.

What You Could've Won | "What You Could've Won" is a song by English indie rock band Milburn and featured on their debut album, "Well Well Well".  Released on 30 October 2006, it was the third single released from the album and charted at #66 in the UK.

What You Want (Evanescence song) | "What You Want" is a song by American rock band Evanescence.  It was released as the lead single from the band's third eponymous album on August 9, 2011 through Wind-up Records.  The song was written by Amy Lee, Terry Balsamo and Tim McCord and produced by Nick Raskulinecz.  Lee described "What You Want" as a departure from the band's previous sound and said she was inspired to write the song by several experiences she had with her fans.  Musically "What You Want" contains a variety of musical elements and it uses drums, guitars, synthesizers and a piano.  Lyrically, the song has a theme of freedom.

It Could Happen to You (song) | "It Could Happen to You" is a popular standard with music by Jimmy Van Heusen and lyrics by Johnny Burke.  The song was written in 1943 and was introduced by Dorothy Lamour in the Paramount musical comedy film, "And the Angels Sing" (1944).
hop1_titles: When You Leave That Way You Can Never Go Back
response.summary_1:
- Fact: [1] — "written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical Follies" (song origin; mentions Phyllis).
- Fact: [1] — the song is "for the character Phyllis" (ties the song to a character in Follies).

Bridge entity and missing/ambiguous facts:
- Bridge entity: Follies (the 1971 musical). Missing fact: the passages do NOT state what Follies "concerns" (its subject/plot/theme). The authoritative article for that musical is not present in the supplied passages.

Prioritized next-hop retrievals to resolve missing element:
1. Fetch the page "Follies (musical)" — extract the lead/lede sentence and the "Synopsis" or "Plot" section (or section titled "Overview") to get a concise statement of what the musical concerns (e.g., subject, setting, central premise).
2. From the same page, fetch the "Themes" or "Background" section (if present) to confirm phrasing like "concerns..." or "is about..." and to capture any alternate concise descriptions.
3. If "Follies (musical)" is ambiguous or missing, fetch "Follies (disambiguation)" and then the specific "Follies (musical)" page; extract lead/synopsis as above.
4. (Optional) Fetch the "Could I Leave You? (song)" page lead/infobox (if separate) to confirm linkage to the same Follies page and the character Phyllis.

No final answer provided here because the supplied passages do not state what the musical concerns.
response.hop2_query: Follies (1971 musical) synopsis plot what is it about?
hop2_docs: I'm Still Here (Follies song) | I'm Still Here is a song written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical "Follies".

The Thing About Men | The Thing About Men is a musical with music by Jimmy Roberts and lyrics and book by Joe DiPietro.  It is based on the 1985 German film Men by Doris Dörrie.  The plot revolves around a love triangle and shows what men will do to keep their pride and love affairs intact.
Could I Leave You? (song) | "Could I Leave You?"  is a song written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical "Follies" for the character Phyllis.
Mad About Opera | Mad About Opera (Italian: "Follie per l'opera" ) is a 1948 Italian musical comedy film directed by Mario Costa.

Castlebourne folly | The Castlebourne folly in the parish of Belbroughton, Worcestershire, England, was built as a sham castle towards the end of the eighteenth century and is similar in age and design to Clent Castle another folly about 2.2 mi away in an adjacent parish of Clent.  In the middle nineteenth century a house, Castlebourne (originally Castle Bourne) was built in a Gothic castle style close to the folly and connected by a wall about 20 yd long and 5 yd high with an archway.  The folly, wall and house are designated as a Grade II listed building by English Heritage.

Folly Fellowship | The Folly Fellowship is a not for profit organisation set up in 1988 as a pressure group to protect, preserve and promote awareness of Britain’s follies, grottoes and garden buildings.  It organises trips throughout the year to follies and holds an annual garden party at a follied garden where the highlight is the cutting of a cake formed in the shape of one of the follies in the garden.  Members also receive an annual Journal, three Magazines a year and a monthly e-Bulletin, each giving information about follies in different depths.  Folly Fellowship members include architects, people who live in follies, people who build follies and other interested persons.

Folly fort | A folly fort or just folly is a specific type of fort built in shallow water, near the shore, in the second half of the 19th century.  The main characteristic of a folly fort was that it was built on the water, very close to the edge, very small, and exclusively for military defense with no intention of being used as normal living quarters.  They were really defense fortifications rather than what were called "forts" at the time, which were on land and much larger.
response.summary_2: "Could I Leave You?" is a song written by Stephen Sondheim for the 1971 musical Follies and is for the character Phyllis. The supplied passages identify the song's origin and character but do not state what the musical Follies concerns.
